title: Television series

infobox.value: ([British English](/wiki/British_English_language "British English language"): **programme**), or simply a **TV show**, is the general reference to any content produced for viewing on a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set") that is transmitted via [over-the-air](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television"), [satellite](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television"), and [cable](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television"), or [distributed digitally](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") on [streaming platforms](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service").[[1]](#cite_note-Willcox-TV-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Pedersen-2025-2) This generally excludes [breaking news](/wiki/Breaking_news "Breaking news") or [advertisements](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement") that are aired between shows or between segments of a show. A regularly recurring show is called a **television series,** and an individual segment of such a series is called an **episode.** Content is produced either in-house on a television stage with multiple cameras or produced by contract with film production companies. Episodes are usually broadcast in annual sets, which are called **seasons** in North America and **series** in other regions. A one-off television show may be called a [television special](/wiki/Television_special "Television special"), while a show with a limited number of episodes is a [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries").[[a]](#cite_note-5) A [television film](/wiki/Television_film "Television film"), or **telefilm**, is a [feature film](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film") produced for broadcast by a [terrestrial](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") or cable network.

A **television show**, **TV program** ([British English](/wiki/British_English_language "British English language"): **programme**), or simply a **TV show**, is the general reference to any content produced for viewing on a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set") that is transmitted via [over-the-air](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television"), [satellite](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television"), and [cable](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television"), or [distributed digitally](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") on [streaming platforms](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service").[[1]](#cite_note-Willcox-TV-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Pedersen-2025-2) This generally excludes [breaking news](/wiki/Breaking_news "Breaking news") or [advertisements](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement") that are aired between shows or between segments of a show. A regularly recurring show is called a **television series,** and an individual segment of such a series is called an **episode.** Content is produced either in-house on a television stage with multiple cameras or produced by contract with film production companies. Episodes are usually broadcast in annual sets, which are called **seasons** in North America and **series** in other regions. A one-off television show may be called a [television special](/wiki/Television_special "Television special"), while a show with a limited number of episodes is a [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries").[[a]](#cite_note-5) A [television film](/wiki/Television_film "Television film"), or **telefilm**, is a [feature film](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film") produced for broadcast by a [terrestrial](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") or cable network.

Television shows by terrestrial and cable networks are most often [scheduled for broadcast](/wiki/Broadcast_programming "Broadcast programming") ahead of time and appear on [electronic guides](/wiki/Electronic_program_guide "Electronic program guide") or other [TV listings](/wiki/TV_listings "TV listings"). The rise of [streaming television](/wiki/Streaming_television "Streaming television"), however, has made television schedules less relevant than in earlier decades. Some programming may be [aired live](/wiki/Live_television "Live television")—that is, events are broadcast at the time they happen rather than at a later time or date—but the vast majority of programming is produced ahead of time. Originally, viewers had no practical way to record a show for later viewing; this changed with the advent of [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video"), first in the form of videotape recorded on [VCRs](/wiki/VCR "VCR") and later in the form of [digital video recorders](/wiki/Digital_video_recorder "Digital video recorder"). Cable television providers began offering certain programming "pay-per-view" or [on-demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand"), with viewers paying a one-time fee to watch a program at a time of their own choosing. Streaming television allows viewers to watch programming at any time with a subscription to the [OTT platform](/wiki/OTT_platform "OTT platform") service.

## History

Main article: [History of television](/wiki/History_of_television "History of television")

The first television shows were experimental, sporadic broadcasts viewable only within a very short range from the [broadcast tower](/wiki/Broadcast_tower "Broadcast tower") starting in the 1930s. Televised events such as the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics") in Germany, the 1937 [coronation of King George VI](/wiki/Coronation_of_King_George_VI_and_Queen_Elizabeth#Television "Coronation of King George VI and Queen Elizabeth") in the United Kingdom, and [David Sarnoff's](/wiki/David_Sarnoff#Early_history_of_television "David Sarnoff") famous introduction at the [1939 New York World's Fair](/wiki/1939_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1939 New York World's Fair") in the United States spurred growth in the medium, but [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") put a halt to development until after the war. The [1947 World Series](/wiki/1947_World_Series "1947 World Series") inspired many Americans to buy their first television set, and then in 1948, the popular radio show *[Texaco Star Theater](/wiki/Texaco_Star_Theater "Texaco Star Theater")* made the move and became the first weekly televised [variety show](/wiki/Variety_show "Variety show"), earning host [Milton Berle](/wiki/Milton_Berle "Milton Berle") the name "Mr. Television", and demonstrating that the medium was a stable, modern form of entertainment that could attract [advertisers](/wiki/Television_advertising "Television advertising"). The first national [live television](/wiki/Live_television "Live television") broadcast in the US took place on September 4, 1951, when President [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman")'s speech at the [Japanese Peace Treaty Conference](/wiki/Treaty_of_San_Francisco "Treaty of San Francisco") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") was transmitted over [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T")'s transcontinental [cable](/wiki/Coaxial_cable "Coaxial cable") and [microwave radio relay](/wiki/Microwave_radio_relay "Microwave radio relay") system to broadcast stations in local markets.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The first national [color](/wiki/Color_television "Color television") broadcast (the 1954 [Tournament of Roses Parade](/wiki/Tournament_of_Roses_Parade "Tournament of Roses Parade")) in the US occurred on January 1, 1954. During the following ten years, most network broadcasts, and nearly all local programming, continued to be in black-and-white. The color transition was announced for the fall of 1965, during which over half of all network prime-time programming would be broadcast in color. The first all-color prime-time season came just one year later. In 1972, the last holdout among daytime network shows converted to color, resulting in the first completely all-color network season.

## Formats and genres

Further information: [List of genres § Film and television genres](/wiki/List_of_genres#Film_and_television_genres "List of genres")

Television shows are more varied than most other forms of media due to the wide variety of formats and genres that can be presented. A show may be [fictional](/wiki/Fictional "Fictional") (as in [comedies](/wiki/Television_comedy "Television comedy"), [animations](/wiki/Animated_series "Animated series") and [dramas](/wiki/Dramatic_programming "Dramatic programming")), or [non-fictional](/wiki/Non-fictional "Non-fictional") (as in [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_television "Documentary television"), [news](/wiki/Television_news "Television news"), and [reality television](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television")). It may be topical (as in the case of a [local](/wiki/Local_programming "Local programming") [newscast](/wiki/Newscast "Newscast") and some [made-for-television](/wiki/Made-for-television "Made-for-television") films), or historical (as in the case of many documentaries and fictional series). They could be primarily [instructional](/wiki/Instructional_television "Instructional television"), [educational](/wiki/Educational_television "Educational television"), or entertaining, as is the case in [situation comedy](/wiki/Situation_comedy "Situation comedy") and [game shows](/wiki/Game_show "Game show").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A drama program usually features a set of actors playing characters in a historical or contemporary setting. The program follows their lives and adventures. Before the 1980s, shows (except for [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera")-type [serials](/wiki/Serial_(radio_and_television) "Serial (radio and television)")) typically remained static without [story arcs](/wiki/Story_arc "Story arc"), and the main characters and premise changed little.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] If some change happened to the characters' lives during the [episode](/wiki/Episode "Episode"), it was usually undone by the end. Due to this, the episodes could be broadcast in any order.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since the 1980s, many series feature progressive change in the plot, the characters, or both. For instance, *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* and *[St. Elsewhere](/wiki/St._Elsewhere "St. Elsewhere")* were two of the first US prime time drama television series to have this kind of dramatic structure,[[8]](#cite_note-9)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] while the later series *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")* further exemplifies such structure in that it had a predetermined story running over its intended five-season run.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2012, it was reported that television was growing into a larger component of major media companies' revenues than film.[[9]](#cite_note-wrap120606-10) Some also noted the increase in quality of some television programs. In 2012, Academy Award-winning film director [Steven Soderbergh](/wiki/Steven_Soderbergh "Steven Soderbergh"), commenting on ambiguity and complexity of character and narrative, stated: "I think those qualities are now being seen on television, and that people who want to see stories that have those kinds of qualities are watching television."[[10]](#cite_note-thr120629-11)

## Production

### Development

#### United States

When a person or company decides to create new content for television broadcast, they develop the show's elements, consisting of the [concept](/wiki/Concept "Concept"), the [characters](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)"), the [crew](/wiki/Television_crew "Television crew"), and the cast. Then they often "pitch" it to the various [networks](/wiki/Television_network "Television network") in an attempt to find one interested enough to order a [prototype](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype") for the first episode of the series, known as a [*pilot*](/wiki/Television_pilot "Television pilot").[[11]](#cite_note-12) [Eric Coleman](/wiki/Eric_Coleman_(producer) "Eric Coleman (producer)"), an animation executive at [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney"), told an interviewer, "One misconception is that it's very difficult to get in and pitch your show, when the truth is that development executives at networks want very much to hear ideas. They want very much to get the word out on what types of shows they're looking for."[[12]](#cite_note-13)

To create the pilot, the structure and team of the whole series must be put together. If audiences respond well to the pilot, the network will pick up the show to air it the next season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Sometimes they save it for mid-season or request rewrites and additional review.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other times, they *pass* entirely, forcing the show's creator to "shop it around" to other networks. Many shows never make it past the pilot stage.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The method of "team writing" is employed on some longer dramatic series (usually running up to a maximum of around 13 episodes). The idea for such a program may be generated "in-house" by one of the networks; it could originate from an independent production company (sometimes a product of both). For instance, the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s long-running soap opera *[EastEnders](/wiki/EastEnders "EastEnders")* is wholly a BBC production, whereas its popular drama [*Life on Mars*](/wiki/Life_on_Mars_(UK_TV_series) "Life on Mars (UK TV series)") was developed by [Kudos](/wiki/Kudos_(production_company) "Kudos (production company)") in association with the broadcaster.

There are still a significant number of programs (usually [sitcoms](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom")) that are built by just one or two writers and a small, close-knit production team. These are "pitched" in the traditional way, but since the creators handle all the writing requirements, there is a run of six or seven episodes per series once approval has been given. Many of the most popular British comedies have been made this way, including *[Monty Python's Flying Circus](/wiki/Monty_Python%27s_Flying_Circus "Monty Python's Flying Circus")* (albeit with an exclusive team of six writer-performers), *[Fawlty Towers](/wiki/Fawlty_Towers "Fawlty Towers")*, *[Blackadder](/wiki/Blackadder "Blackadder")* and [*The Office*](/wiki/The_Office_(British_TV_series) "The Office (British TV series)").

Tamvisio's [camera operators](/wiki/Camera_operator "Camera operator") film a television program at Frenckell's studio on January 2, 1965, in [Tampere](/wiki/Tampere "Tampere"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland").

The [production company](/wiki/Production_company "Production company") is often separate from the broadcaster. The [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer"), often the show's creator, is in charge of running the show. They pick the [crew](/wiki/Television_crew "Television crew") and help cast the actors, approve and sometimes write series plots—some even write or direct major episodes—while various other producers help to ensure that the show runs smoothly. Very occasionally, the executive producer will cast themselves in the show. As with [filmmaking](/wiki/Filmmaking "Filmmaking") or other [electronic media](/wiki/Electronic_media "Electronic media") production, producing of an individual episode can be divided into three parts: [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production"), [principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography"), and [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production").

### Pre-production

Main article: [Pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Arthur_Schlesinger%2C_Jr._NBC-TV_program_1951.JPG/250px-Arthur_Schlesinger%2C_Jr._NBC-TV_program_1951.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arthur_Schlesinger,_Jr._NBC-TV_program_1951.JPG)

Arthur Schlesinger Jr.

Pre-production begins when a script is approved. A [director](/wiki/Television_director "Television director") is chosen to plan the episode's final look. Pre-production tasks include storyboarding; construction of sets, props, and costumes; casting guest stars; budgeting; acquiring resources like lighting, special effects, stunts, etc. Once the show is planned, it must then be scheduled: scenes are often filmed out of sequence, and guest actors or even regulars may only be available at certain times. Sometimes the principal photography of different episodes must be done at the same time, complicating the schedule (a guest star might shoot scenes from two episodes on the same afternoon). Complex scenes are translated from storyboard to [animatics](/wiki/Traditional_animation#Animatic "Traditional animation") to further clarify the action. Scripts are adjusted to meet altering requirements.

Some shows have a small stable of directors, but also usually rely on outside directors. Given the time constraints of broadcasting, a single show might have two or three episodes in pre-production, one or two episodes in principal photography, and a few more in various stages of post-production. The task of directing is complex enough that a single director can usually not work on more than one episode or show at a time, hence the need for multiple directors.

### Principal photography

Main article: [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography")

Principal photography is the actual filming of the episode. Director, actors, and crew gather at a [television studio](/wiki/Television_studio "Television studio") or on location for filming or [videoing](/wiki/Video_production "Video production") a scene. A scene is further divided into shots, which should be planned during pre-production. Depending on scheduling, a scene may be shot in non-sequential order of the story. Conversations may be filmed twice from different [camera angles](/wiki/Camera_angle "Camera angle"), often using stand-ins, so one actor might perform all their lines in one set of shots, and then the other side of the conversation is filmed from the opposite perspective. To complete a production on time, a second unit may be filming a different scene on another set or location at the same time, using a different set of actors, an assistant director, and a second unit crew. A [director of photography](/wiki/Director_of_photography "Director of photography") supervises the lighting of each shot to ensure consistency.

Live events are usually covered by Outside Broadcast crews using mobile television studios, known as scanners or OB trucks. Although varying greatly depending on the era and subject covered, these trucks were normally crewed by up to 15 skilled operators and production personnel. In the UK for most of the 20th century, the BBC was the preeminent provider of outside broadcast coverage. BBC crews worked on almost every major event, including Royal weddings and funerals, major political and sporting events, and even drama programs.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

### Post-production

Main article: [Post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production")

Once principal photography is complete, producers coordinate tasks to begin the [video editing](/wiki/Video_editing "Video editing"). Visual and [digital video effects](/wiki/Digital_video_effect "Digital video effect") are added to the film; this is often outsourced to companies specializing in these areas. Often music is performed with the conductor using the film as a time reference (other musical elements may be previously recorded). An [editor](/wiki/Film_editing#Film_Editor "Film editing") cuts the various pieces of film together, adds the [musical score](/wiki/Incidental_music "Incidental music") and effects, determines scene transitions, and assembles the completed show.

## Budgets and revenues

Most television networks throughout the world are 'commercial', dependent on selling advertising time or acquiring [sponsors](/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Broadcasting executives' main concern over their programming is [audience](/wiki/Audience "Audience") size.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the past, the number of 'free to air' stations was restricted by the availability of [channel](/wiki/Television_channel "Television channel") frequencies, but [cable TV](/wiki/Cable_TV "Cable TV") (outside the United States, [satellite television](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television")) technology has allowed an expansion in the number of channels available to viewers (sometimes at premium rates) in a much more competitive environment.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the United States, the average broadcast network drama costs $3 million an episode to produce, while cable dramas cost $2 million on average.[[15]](#cite_note-nyt100404-16) The [pilot](/wiki/Television_pilot "Television pilot") episode may be more expensive than a regular episode.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2004, [*Lost*](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")'s two-hour pilot cost $10 to $14 million, in 2008, [*Fringe*](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")'s two-hour pilot cost $10 million, and in 2010, *[Boardwalk Empire](/wiki/Boardwalk_Empire "Boardwalk Empire")* was $18 million for the first episode. In 2011, [*Game of Thrones*](/wiki/Winter_Is_Coming "Winter Is Coming") was $5 to $10 million, [*Pan Am*](/wiki/Pan_Am_(TV_series) "Pan Am (TV series)") cost an estimated $10 million, while [*Terra Nova*](/wiki/Terra_Nova_(TV_series) "Terra Nova (TV series)")'s two-hour pilot was between $10 and $20 million.[[16]](#cite_note-thr110926-17)[[17]](#cite_note-nyt110828-18)

Many scripted [network television](/wiki/Network_television "Network television") shows in the United States are financed through deficit financing: a studio finances the production cost of a show and a network pays a license fee to the studio for the right to air the show. This license fee does not cover the show's production costs, leading to the deficit. Although the studio does not make its money back in the original airing of the show, it retains ownership of the show. This allows the studio to make its money back and earn a profit through [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") and sales of [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-rays](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"). This system places most of the financial risk on the studios; however, a hit show in the [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") and home video markets can more than make up for the misses. Although deficit financing places minimal financial risk on the networks, they lose out on the future profits of big hits since they are only licensing the shows.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Costs are recouped mainly by advertising revenues for broadcast networks and some cable channels, while other cable channels depend on subscriptions. In general, advertisers, and consequently networks that depend on advertising, are more interested in the number of viewers within the 18–49 age range than in the total number of viewers.[[19]](#cite_note-demos-20)[[20]](#cite_note-pocketbook-21) Advertisers are willing to pay more to advertise on shows successful with young adults because they watch less television and are harder to reach.[[21]](#cite_note-reason-22) According to *Advertising Age*, during the 2007–08 season, *[Grey's Anatomy](/wiki/Grey%27s_Anatomy "Grey's Anatomy")* was able to charge $419,000 per commercial, compared to only $248,000 for a commercial during *[CSI](/wiki/CSI:_Crime_Scene_Investigation "CSI: Crime Scene Investigation")*, despite CSI having almost five million more viewers on average.[[22]](#cite_note-demos2-23) Due to its strength with younger viewers, *[Friends](/wiki/Friends_(TV_series) "Friends (TV series)")* was able to charge almost three times as much for a commercial as *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*, even though the two series had similar total viewer numbers at that time.[[19]](#cite_note-demos-20) *[Glee](/wiki/Glee_(TV_series) "Glee (TV series)")* and *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)")* drew fewer total viewers than *[NCIS](/wiki/NCIS_(TV_series) "NCIS (TV series)")* during the 2009–10 season, but earned an average of $272,694 and $213,617 respectively, compared to $150,708 for NCIS.[[23]](#cite_note-estadrates-24)

After production, the show is handed over to the [television network](/wiki/Television_network "Television network"), which sends it out to its [affiliate](/wiki/Network_affiliate "Network affiliate") [stations](/wiki/Television_station "Television station"), which broadcast it in the specified [broadcast programming](/wiki/Broadcast_programming "Broadcast programming") time slot. If the [Nielsen ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") are good, the show is kept alive as long as possible. If not, the show is usually [canceled](/wiki/Cancellation_(television) "Cancellation (television)"). The show's creators are then left to shop around for remaining episodes, and the possibility of future episodes, on other networks. On especially successful series, the producers sometimes call a halt to a series on their own like *[Seinfeld](/wiki/Seinfeld "Seinfeld")*, *[The Cosby Show](/wiki/The_Cosby_Show "The Cosby Show")*, *[Corner Gas](/wiki/Corner_Gas "Corner Gas")*, and [*M\*A\*S\*H*](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)") and end it with a concluding episode, which sometimes is a big [series finale](/wiki/Series_finale "Series finale").

On rare occasions, a series that has not attracted particularly high ratings and has been canceled can be given a reprieve if [home video](/wiki/Home_video "Home video") viewership has been particularly strong. This has happened in the cases of *[Family Guy](/wiki/Family_Guy "Family Guy")* in the US and [*Peep Show*](/wiki/Peep_Show_(British_TV_series) "Peep Show (British TV series)") in the UK.

In the United States, if the show is popular or lucrative, and a minimum number of episodes ([usually 100](/wiki/100_episodes "100 episodes")) have been made, it can go into [broadcast syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication"), where rights to broadcast the program are then resold for cash or put into a barter exchange (offered to an outlet for free in exchange for airing additional commercials elsewhere in the station's broadcast day).

## Seasons/series/strand

The terminology used to define a set of [episodes](/wiki/Episode "Episode") produced for a television series varies from country to country.

### North American usage

See also: [Serial (radio and television)](/wiki/Serial_(radio_and_television) "Serial (radio and television)")

In North American television, a series is a connected set of television program episodes that run under the same title, possibly spanning many seasons. During the 1950s, it was common for television seasons to consist of more than 30 episodes—however, the average length has been declining since.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Until the 1980s, most new programs for the US [broadcast networks](/wiki/Broadcast_network "Broadcast network") debuted in the "fall season", which ran from September through March and nominally contained 24 to 26 episodes. These episodes were rebroadcast during the spring (or summer) season, from April through August. Because of [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") and the Nielsen [sweeps](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings"), the "fall" season now normally extends to May. Thus, a "full season" on a broadcast network now usually runs from September through May for at least 22 episodes.[[25]](#cite_note-NetworkShortOrders-26)

A full season is sometimes split into two separate units with a hiatus around the end of the calendar year, such as the first season of [*Jericho*](/wiki/Jericho_(2006_TV_series) "Jericho (2006 TV series)") on CBS. When this split occurs, the last half of the episodes are sometimes referred to with the letter B as in "The last nine episodes (of *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")*) will be part of what is being called either 'Season 6, Part 2' or 'Season 6B'",[[26]](#cite_note-27) or "*[Futurama](/wiki/Futurama "Futurama")* is splitting its seasons similar to how *[South Park](/wiki/South_Park "South Park")* does, doing half a season at a time, so this is season 6B for them."[[27]](#cite_note-28) Since the 1990s, these shorter seasons also have been referred to as "split" or "half" seasons, which is done to increase profits, as seen with shows such as *[The Witcher](/wiki/The_Witcher_(TV_series) "The Witcher (TV series)")*.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Since at least the 2000s, new broadcast television series are often ordered (funded) for just the first 10 to 13 episodes, to gauge audience interest. If a series is popular, the network places a "back nine order" and the season is completed to the regular 20 to 26 episodes. An established series that is already popular, however, will typically receive an immediate full-season order at the outset of the season. A [midseason replacement](/wiki/Midseason_replacement "Midseason replacement") is a less-expensive short-run show of generally 10 to 13 episodes designed to take the place of an original series that failed to garner an audience and has not been picked up. A "series finale" is the last show of the series before the show is no longer produced. (In the UK, it means the end of a season, what is known in the United States as a "*season* finale".) Streaming services time finales to the next quarter to induce consumers to renew at least one more quarter.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

A standard television season in the United States runs predominantly during [autumn](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn").[[30]](#cite_note-:0-31) During the summer months of June through roughly mid-September, network schedules typically feature reruns of their flagship programs, first-run series with lower rating expectations, and other specials. First-run scripted series are typically shorter and of a lower profile than those aired during the main season and can also include [limited series](#Miniseries,_limited_series,_and_event_series) events. [Reality](/wiki/Reality_show "Reality show") and [game shows](/wiki/Game_show "Game show") have also been fixtures of the schedule.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-31)

In Canada, the commercial networks air most American programming in tandem with the American television season, but their original Canadian shows follow a model closer to British than American television production. Due to the smaller production budgets available in Canada, a Canadian show's season normally runs to a maximum of 13 episodes rather than 20 or more, although an exceptionally popular series such as *[Corner Gas](/wiki/Corner_Gas "Corner Gas")* or *[Murdoch Mysteries](/wiki/Murdoch_Mysteries "Murdoch Mysteries")* might receive 20-episode orders in later seasons. Canadian shows do not normally receive "back nine" extensions within the same season, however; even a popular series simply ends for the year when the original production order has finished airing, and an expanded order of more than 13 episodes is applied to the next season's renewal order rather than an extension of the current season. Only the public [CBC Television](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television") normally schedules Canadian-produced programming throughout the year; the commercial networks typically now avoid scheduling Canadian productions to air in the fall, as such shows commonly get lost amid the publicity onslaught of the American fall season. Instead, Canadian-produced shows on the commercial networks typically air either in the winter as mid-season replacements for canceled American shows or in the summer (which may also improve their chances of being picked up by an American network for a summer run).[[31]](#cite_note-32)

#### Miniseries, limited series, and event series

While network orders for 13- or 22-episode seasons are still pervasive in the television industry, several shows have deviated from this traditional trend. Written to be closed-ended and of shorter length than other shows, they are marketed with a variety of terms.

* [Miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries"): A very short, closed-ended series, typically six or more hours in two or more parts (nights), similar to an extended [television movie](/wiki/Television_film "Television film"). Many early miniseries were adaptations of popular novels of the day, such as [*The National Dream*](/wiki/The_National_Dream_(miniseries) "The National Dream (miniseries)") (1974), [*Roots*](/wiki/Roots_(1977_miniseries) "Roots (1977 miniseries)") (1977), and [*North and South*](/wiki/North_and_South_(miniseries) "North and South (miniseries)") (1985). In recent years, as described by several television executives interviewed by *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")*, the term *miniseries* has grown to have negative [connotations](/wiki/Connotation "Connotation") within the industry, having become associated with [melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama")-heavy works that were commonly produced under the format, while *limited series* or *event series* receive higher respect.[[32]](#cite_note-miniseries-33)

* [Limited series](/wiki/Limited-run_series "Limited-run series"): Distinct from miniseries in that the production is seen to have potential to be renewed, but without the requirement of it having as many episodes as a typical order per season. [*Under the Dome*](/wiki/Under_the_Dome_(TV_series) "Under the Dome (TV series)"), *[Killer Women](/wiki/Killer_Women "Killer Women")*, and [*Luther*](/wiki/Luther_(TV_series) "Luther (TV series)") were marketed as limited series. Individual season-length stories of [anthology series](/wiki/Anthology_series#Television "Anthology series") such as *[American Horror Story](/wiki/American_Horror_Story "American Horror Story")*, [*Fargo*](/wiki/Fargo_(TV_series) "Fargo (TV series)"), and *[True Detective](/wiki/True_Detective "True Detective")* are also described as "limited series". The [Primetime Emmys](/wiki/Primetime_Emmys "Primetime Emmys") have had to make numerous changes to their [miniseries/limited series category](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Limited_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Limited Series") to accommodate [anthology](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology") and other limited series.[[33]](#cite_note-Variety-Limited-34)
* Event series: Largely considered a marketing term, falling under the general category of [event television](/wiki/Event_television "Event television"). The term can be applied to almost any new, short-run series, such as *[24: Live Another Day](/wiki/24:_Live_Another_Day "24: Live Another Day")*. It has also been used to describe [game shows](/wiki/Game_show "Game show") like *[The Million Second Quiz](/wiki/The_Million_Second_Quiz "The Million Second Quiz")* which aired for just two weeks.[[32]](#cite_note-miniseries-33)

### UK, Ireland and Australia usage

In the United Kingdom and other countries, these sets of episodes are referred to as a "series". In Australia, the broadcasting may be different from North American usage. The terms *series* and *season* are both used and are the same. For example, *[Battlestar Galactica](/wiki/Battlestar_Galactica "Battlestar Galactica")* has an original series as well as a remake, both are considered a different series, each with their own number of individual seasons.

Australian television does not follow "seasons" in the way that US television does; for example, there is no "[fall](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn") season" or "fall schedule". For many years, popular night-time dramas in Australia would run for much of the year, and would only go into recess during the summer period (December to February, as Australia is in the Southern Hemisphere), when ratings are not taken. Therefore, popular dramas would usually run from February through November each year. This schedule was used in the 1970s for popular dramas, including *[Number 96](/wiki/Number_96_(TV_series) "Number 96 (TV series)")*. Many drama series, such as *[McLeod's Daughters](/wiki/McLeod%27s_Daughters "McLeod's Daughters")*, have received between 22 and 32 episodes per season.

Typically, [soap operas](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera"), which have always run in season format in Australia, such as *[Home and Away](/wiki/Home_and_Away "Home and Away")*, would usually begin a new season in late January, while the season finale would air in late November, as the show is off air for two months, or sometimes longer, depending on the schedule. In recent years,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] a new season would begin in early February, and the season finale would broadcast in early December. Since *Home and Away*'s inception, it normally receives 230 episodes per season. Some seasons have seen between 205 and 235 episodes commissioned. During the [Olympics](/wiki/Olympics "Olympics"), *Home and Away* would often go on hiatus, which was referred to as an "Olympic cliffhanger". Therefore, the number of episodes would decrease. Australian [situation comedy](/wiki/Situation_comedy "Situation comedy") series' seasons are approximately 13 episodes long and premiere any time between February and November.

British television programmes have tended toward shorter series in recent years. For example, the [first series](/wiki/Doctor_Who_season_1_(1963%E2%80%931964) "Doctor Who season 1 (1963–1964)") of long-running [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") show *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* in 1963 featured forty-two 25‑minute episodes, and continued with a similar number each year until it was reduced to twenty-five [for 1970](/wiki/Doctor_Who_season_7 "Doctor Who season 7") to accommodate changes in production and significantly reducing the actors' workload) and continued to 1984. [For 1985](/wiki/Doctor_Who_season_22 "Doctor Who season 22") fewer but longer episodes were shown, but even after a return to shorter episodes in 1986, lack of support within the BBC meant fewer episodes were commissioned leading to only fourteen 25‑minute episodes up to [those in 1989](/wiki/Doctor_Who_season_26 "Doctor Who season 26") after which it was cancelled. The revival of *Doctor Who* from 2005 has comprised thirteen 45‑minute installments.

There are some series in the UK that have a larger number of episodes, for example [*Waterloo Road*](/wiki/Waterloo_Road_(TV_series) "Waterloo Road (TV series)") started with 8 to 12 episodes, but from series three onward it increased to twenty episodes and series seven will contain 30 episodes. Recently, US non-cable networks have also begun to experiment with shorter series for some programs, particularly reality shows, such as [*Survivor*](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)"). They often air two series per year, resulting in roughly the same number of episodes per year as a drama.

This is a reduction from the 1950s, in which many US shows (e.g. *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")*) had between 29 and 39 episodes per season. Actual storytelling time within a commercial television hour has also gradually reduced over the years, from 50 minutes out of every 60 to the current 44 (and even less on some networks), beginning in the early 21st century.

The usage of "season" and "series" differ for DVD and Blu-ray releases in both Australia and the UK. In Australia, many locally produced shows are termed differently on home video releases. For example, a set of the television drama series *[Packed to the Rafters](/wiki/Packed_to_the_Rafters "Packed to the Rafters")* or [*Wentworth*](/wiki/Wentworth_(TV_series) "Wentworth (TV series)") is referred to as "season" ("The Complete First Season", etc.), whereas drama series such as [*Tangle*](/wiki/Tangle_(TV_series) "Tangle (TV series)") are known as a "series" ("Series 1", etc.). British-produced programmes such as *[Mrs. Brown's Boys](/wiki/Mrs._Brown%27s_Boys "Mrs. Brown's Boys")* are referred to as "season" in Australia for the DVD and Blu-ray releases.

In the UK and Ireland, most programmes are referred to as 'series' while 'season' is starting to be used for some US and international releases.

### Egypt

The 1980s and 1990s was the golden age of television miniseries attracting millions of Egyptians. For example, *[The Family of Mr Shalash](/wiki/The_Family_of_Mr_Shalash "The Family of Mr Shalash")* miniseries, starring [Salah Zulfikar](/wiki/Salah_Zulfikar "Salah Zulfikar") and [Laila Taher](/wiki/Laila_Taher "Laila Taher"), was the highest rated at the time.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

## Running time

In the United States, dramas produced for hour-long time slots typically are 37–42 minutes in length (excluding advertisements), while [sitcoms](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") produced for 30-minute time slots typically are 18–21 minutes long. There are exceptions: subscription-based cable TV channels, such as [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"), Starz, Cinemax, and Showtime, have episodes that are 45–48 minutes long, similar to the UK. Audience opinions of length have varied due to factors such as [content overload](/wiki/Information_overload#Information_Age "Information overload").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In Britain, dramas typically run from 46 to 48 minutes on commercial channels, and 57–59 minutes on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"). Half-hour programs are around 22 minutes on commercial channels and around 28 minutes on the BBC. The longer duration on the BBC is due to the lack of advertising breaks.

In France, most television shows (whether dramas, game shows or documentaries) have a duration of 52 minutes. This is the same on nearly all French networks (TF1, France 2, France 5, M6, Canal+, etc.).[[36]](#cite_note-37)

The episode runtime of television shows produced for [streaming platforms](/wiki/Streaming_platform "Streaming platform"), such as [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") and [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"), can vary from just under 30 minutes to over one hour long.[[37]](#cite_note-Volpe-38)[[38]](#cite_note-Schneider-39) Internet-based series with episode runtimes of less than 25 minutes are considered [web series](/wiki/Web_series "Web series").[[37]](#cite_note-Volpe-38)
